segments.s331-g41: که here opens a rhetorical question meaning 'who'. داند is the classical 3rd-person singular present 'knows'. مگر functions as an exception marker 'except/ save' and attaches to the following noun phrase; کردگار سپهر 'the Maker/Creator of the heavens' is the exception (the one who alone might know). Word order is poetic (verb then subject/exception clause follows).
segments.s331-g42: نماینده is a noun meaning 'manifestor/representative/agent' used predicatively (no copula) to describe the Creator just mentioned. کام و آرام و مهر is a coordinated list of abstract nouns ('desire, calm/peace, and love') linked by و; together they form the content that the Creator is said to 'manifest' or 'bring'.
segments.s331-g43: سخنهای او ('his words') is the subject phrase. نیست is the negative copula/existential 'is not'; اندر نهفت is a predicative compound meaning 'hidden inside/concealed within' (نهفت is a past-participle/adjectival form). Together the line says his words are not concealed.
segments.s331-g44: نداند کس is an inverted (poetic) order meaning 'no one knows' (کس here takes the 'no one' sense because the verb is negative). او را marks 'him' as the (definite) object. به آفاق is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in/throughout the horizons', and جفت ('pair' / 'match') names what is not known to exist for him — i.e. 'no one knows a match for him across the horizons.'
segments.s331-g17: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a past clause; دیوان is the plural noun 'divs' (the agents) serving as subject; ببستند is a past tense verb (3rd person plural) 'they bound/tied'; کاووس + را is the definite/accusative object (را marks Kaavus as the patient). The clause literally reads 'When the divs bound Kaavus.'
segments.s331-g18: A second temporal clause introduced by چو 'when'; گودرز is a proper name followed by the epithet گردنکش 'headstrong/rebellious' (adjective follows the noun). و links the next name; طوس را is the coordinated object with را marking Tous as definite/accusative — the construction implies that Goudarz (described as headstrong) and Tous were likewise treated (bound).
segments.s331-g19: تهمتن (an epithet for the hero, used as a name) is the subject. چو + بشنید forms a temporal subordinate clause 'when he heard (it)'. تنها is an adverb 'alone', and برفت is an older past-tense verb 'he went off/left' (بر- + رفت), so the clause means 'Tahmatan, when he heard it, went off alone.'
segments.s331-g20: به مازندران is a prepositional phrase 'to/into Mazandaran'. روی بنهاد is a verbal phrase: روی (literally 'face') + بنهاد (past of نهادن, 'placed/put') used idiomatically as 'he set/turned his face (toward...)'. تفت here is an adverbial/manner word in the manuscript (indicating manner/haste) — the lexical list offered doesn't match this use, but syntactically it modifies the motion ('he set his face toward Mazandaran').
segments.s331-g21: A noun list joined by و 'and' (بیابان, تاریکی, دیو, شیر) enumerating hostile elements or dangers. There is no verb in this segment; it functions as a series of nouns that will be linked to the following descriptive phrase.
segments.s331-g22: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' linking back to the previous list; جادوی is adjectival/relational 'magical, of magic' modifying what follows; و joins another description; اژدهای is 'dragon' (noun) and دلیر is the adjective 'brave' following the noun. Together the phrase characterizes the threats just listed as 'that magic and the brave dragon.'
segments.s331-g23: بدان is a prepositional/compound marker here meaning 'by/with that' (instrumental linkage) followed by رنج و تیمار 'hardship and care' as the instrumental phrase. ببرید is past (3rd pl.) of بردن 'they took/carried/cleared' and راه is the direct object 'the road' — so 'by those hardships and cares they took the road.'
segments.s331-g24: به مازندران is again 'to/into Mazandaran'; شد is a past auxiliary/verb meaning here roughly 'arrived/came' (classical usage where شد marks the event); به نزدیک شاه is a locative/adverbial phrase 'to the proximity of the king' — together 'they/he reached Mazandaran near the king.'
segments.s331-g9: چو + past verb (بشنید) forms a temporal clause 'when he heard...'. دستان here is a poetic plural meaning 'tales/praises/songs' rather than literal 'hands', and it is modified by خسرو پرست ('Khosrow‑worshipper') — i.e. 'the praises/songs of the Khosrow‑worshipper'. Word order is temporal marker → verb → object phrase.
segments.s331-g10: زمین را is the definite object (را marks the direct object) of ببوسید ('he kissed' — 3rd person past). و simply coordinates two actions. برپای جست is an idiomatic verbal phrase: برپای (on the feet/standing) + جست (past of جستن 'to leap'), meaning 'he sprang to his feet' or 'rose suddenly'.
segments.s331-g11: چنین گفت is a reporting formula 'thus he said' introducing direct speech. کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing شهریار جهان ('sovereign/king of the world'); the vocative phrase follows the reporting phrase in this poetic word order.
segments.s331-g12: سزد is an impersonal present/present-subjunctive meaning 'it is fitting' or 'it behooves'. کآرزوها is the object phrase 'that (the) wishes/desires' (ک = classical attachment of که 'that'), followed by ندارم 'I do not have' (1st person present). نهان functions adverbially/adjectivally as 'hidden/secret' — together: 'it is fitting that I have no secret wishes.'
segments.s331-g13: تو دانی is present 'you know'. که introduces a content clause (what follows). Inside the clause, the order is ROSTAM + به ایران (direction/locale) + چه + کرد, i.e. 'what did Rostam do in Iran' — پژوهشی 'چه' is the interrogative/content-word placed before the verb as is normal in Persian embedded clauses.
segments.s331-g14: This is a series of prepositional phrases introduced by repeated به ('in/on/at') enumerating the spheres of Rostam's actions: به رزم ('in/at war'), به بزم ('at the banquet/feast'), به ننگ ('in disgrace/shame') and نبرد ('in battle/struggle'). The repeated به + noun pattern is parallel and idiomatic in classical Persian.
segments.s331-g15: چو ... کی شد forms a temporal clause 'when it happened that...' — here 'چو کاووس کی شد به مازندران' means 'when Kavus went/arrived at Mazandaran' or 'when Kavus set out for Mazandaran'. شد is the past verb 'became/happened/arrived' and به marks direction toward مازندران.
segments.s331-g16: رهی دور is a noun + adjective 'a distant/long road' (رهی here notionally 'on the road / route'), and فرسنگهای گران is a parallel noun phrase 'heavy/weighty (i.e. burdensome or long) parasangs' — the line describes the journey as lengthy and arduous. The coordinating و links the two descriptive phrases.
segments.s331-g1: به ایرانیان گفت places the indirect object before the verb: به ایرانیان = “to the Iranians,” گفت is the past-tense verb “said.” هنگام من is a genitive/possessive phrase ‘my time/occasion’ with the possessed noun (هنگام) followed by the possessor (من); it links as the topic of what he is announcing.
segments.s331-g2: فراز آمد is a short poetic clause meaning ‘rose / came up’ (فراز ‘on high/above’) with آمد ‘came’ as the main past verb. و تازه شد کام من is coordinate: و = ‘and’; تازه شد is ‘became fresh/renewed’ (شد here = ‘became’); کام من = ‘my desire/wish’ (noun then possessor). The verb شد takes کام as its logical subject and the possessive من follows it in normal Persian order.
segments.s331-g3: بخواهید is a 2nd-person plural exhortation/subjunctive form meaning ‘ask/want (you should ask)’ and here functions imperatively/polite imperative. چیزی که باید ز من is a relative phrase: چیزی = ‘something/anything,’ که باید = ‘that you ought/should (ask),’ ز من = ‘from me’ (poetic form of از). The relative clause determines what to request of the speaker.
segments.s331-g4: که آغاز یک جملهٔ وابسته است: که آمد پراگندن انجمن is a relative/purpose clause attached to the preceding من (i.e. ‘I who have come…’). آمد is the past ‘came,’ and آمد + infinitive (پراگندن) expresses purpose here: ‘(I) came to scatter…’. پراگندن انجمن places the infinitive ‘to scatter’ immediately before its object انجمن (assembly) in a poetic inversion.
segments.s331-g5: همه مهتران is the subject ‘all the elders/chief men’ (مهتران here = the great/important men). زار و گریان شدند is the compound predicate: زار (lamenting/wretched) و گریان (weeping) with شدند (past plural of شدن) forming ‘became/were’ — a standard way in Persian to say ‘they were lamenting and crying.’
segments.s331-g6: ز درد شهنشاه is a causal/agent phrase ‘because of the king’s pain’ (ز = ‘from/because of’, درد = sorrow/pain, followed by the possessor شهنشاه). بریان شدند is a metaphorical predicate: بریان literally ‘roasted/burnt’ but here means ‘consumed (by passion/grief)’ with شدند again marking past plural ‘they became/ were.’
segments.s331-g7: همی گفت is an iterative/progressive past ‘he kept saying’ or ‘he would say’ (همی marks continuous/habitual aspect). هرکس که‌ای شهریار = a vocative/exclamatory phrase: هرکس ‘whoever’ and که‌ای (archaic contraction of که + ای) with شهریار ‘O king’ — an address to the ruler; the clause is introductory to the question that follows.
segments.s331-g8: که را مانی این تاج را یادگار is an interrogative clause. که را = ‘to whom’ (که 'who' with object marker را placed after it for emphasis), مانی here is the proper name Mani (vocative: addressing the man Mani), این تاج را = ‘this crown’ with را marking it as the definite object, and یادگار = ‘as a memento/keepsake.’ The word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect, but the sense is: ‘Mani, to whom will you give this crown as a keepsake?’
segments.s331-g33: وز آن پس is a temporal phrase 'from/and after that' (poetic). کجا is an interrogative/adverb 'where/wherever' used to set the scope, رزم is the noun 'battle' placed before the person (typical Persian noun+agent order), کاموس is a proper name here, and کرد is the simple past of کردن used as a light verb meaning 'did/waged'. The clause literally reads 'and from that after where battle Kāmos did' → 'from then on, wherever Kāmos fought'.
segments.s331-g34: به مردی is an object/goal phrase 'to/at a man' (indefinite مردی). به ابر اندر آورد گرد is a verbal phrase: اندر 'in/within' is archaic/poetic; آورد is past 'brought/put', and گرد here completes the verbal idea (literally 'brought (him) round/into') — in context it describes sending or hurling a man into the clouds. Word order puts repeated preposition به before different objects.
segments.s331-g35: ز کردار او is a genitive phrase 'about/from his deed(s)' (ز is poetic 'from/about'). چند modifies سخن and means 'a few/several', and رانم is an archaic 1st-person verbal form meaning 'I will put/utter/drive forth' (here: 'I say' or 'I will relate') — so the segment = 'From his deeds I will say a few words.'
segments.s331-g36: که introduces the reason/explanation clause 'for/that'. هم here means 'even', داستانها is the plural 'the tales/stories', نیاید is a negative present (literary) 'do not/does not come', and به بن is an idiomatic phrase 'to the root/base' meaning 'reach the heart/essence' or 'come to an end'. The clause states the reason why only a little will be said.
segments.s331-g37: اگر is the conditional 'if'. شاه سیر آمد is a classical phrasing: سیر 'satiety/tiring' with آمد 'came' = 'became sated/tired' (i.e. 'grew weary'), and از تاج و گاه means 'of/with respect to crown and throne' (از governs the following nouns). The whole is the protasis of a conditional clause.
segments.s331-g38: چه ماند is a rhetorical question 'what remains/what would be left'. بدین is the contracted/prepositional form (ب + دین) = 'for this' or 'to this' (archaic spelling); شیردل نیک‌خواه is a compound epithet 'lion-hearted, well-wisher' (two adjectives/nouns together describing the person). ماند is the past/stative verb 'remained' used here in a hypothetical sense following the شرطِ اگر.
segments.s331-g39: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed expression 'he gave such an answer / answered thus' — داد is the past of دادن 'gave', پاسـخ is the direct object 'answer'. که introduces the content of that answer. کردار اوی is a noun phrase 'his conduct/deed' with اوی as the archaic possessive 'his'.
segments.s331-g40: به نزدیک ما is a dative/locative phrase 'to/near us' meaning 'for us / in our regard'. رنج و تیمار اوی lists two abstract nouns 'toil/suffering and care' qualified by the possessive اوی 'his' (i.e. 'his suffering and care' or 'suffering for him and his care'). The construction ties these nouns as the content of the reported answer.
segments.s331-g25: بدرید is a past/action verb (poetic form with بـ- prefix) meaning 'they tore/cut.' پهلوی دیو سپید is the definite object phrase 'the flank of the white demon' (پهلوی = flank/side, دیو = demon, سپید post-modifies diو as adjective 'white'). Word order here places the verb before the object for rhythmic/poetic effect.
segments.s331-g26: This is a verbless descriptive continuation referring to the demon's interior: جگرگاه names the inmost part/heart, followed by a paratactic list of descriptors/metaphors (پولاد, غندی, و بید). پولاد is used adjectivally/nominally as 'steel'; غندی and بید are listed as further qualities. The verb from the previous segment supplies the action for this noun phrase.
segments.s331-g27: This clause is active and narrative: سر سنجه را ناگه از تن بکند literally 'he suddenly tore the sanjeh's head from the body.' سر (head) + سنجه (the item/person called 'sanjeh' or a weighing-implement) is marked by را as definite object; ناگه 'suddenly' modifies the verb بکند. بکند is a poetic past/preterite form ('he tore/removed').
segments.s331-g28: خروشش is a noun with the possessive suffix -ش ('his roar/cry'). برآمد is a past verb 'arose/issued forth.' به + ابر بلند functions as a prepositional phrase 'into / up to the high cloud' (به here = towards/into; ابر 'cloud' modified by بلند 'high'). The clause describes the sound travelling upward.
segments.s331-g29: چو here means 'when' (introducing a temporal/illustrative clause). The proper name سهراب is followed by the appositive/epithet فرزند ('the son'). کاندر (a fused archaic locative 'in/within') + جهان gives 'in the world.' Together: 'When Sohrab the son was in the world...'.
segments.s331-g30: کسی را نبود is a common negative existential construction 'there was no one' (کسی here used in the negative sense). را is the object/topic marker attached to کسی; نبود is past 'was not.' از کهان و مهان is a partitive/associative phrase 'among the ancients/elders and the guests/nobles' (poetically: 'among the great and noble'), specifying the classes among whom no equal existed.
segments.s331-g31: بکشت is an archaic/perf.-style verb form here meaning 'he killed' (poetic past). از پی 'in pursuit of' or 'following' introduces the motive, کین 'vengeance' is the object of that pursuit, and کاووس شاه ('Kavus the king') names the agent/beneficiary — the phrase reads 'he killed (him) in pursuit of King Kavus's vengeance' or 'in pursuit of vengeance, King Kavus caused the killing.' Word order is compact and typical of epic poetry.
segments.s331-g32: ز دردش 'from his pain' gives the cause; بگرید is past 'he wept/laments' (3rd person). همی is an adverb meaning 'continually/always,' and سال و ماه 'year and month' is a standard poetic pair meaning 'for years and months' (a long time). The whole clause states that, because of that grief, the king wept continuously.

sentences.s331-s21: Who knows, save the Creator of the heavens, (who is) the bringer of desire, calm, and love?
sentences.s331-s22: His words are not hidden within; no one knows a match for him across the horizons.
sentences.s331-s9: When the divs bound Kaavus, they also bound the rebellious Goudarz and Tous.
sentences.s331-s10: When Tahmatan heard it, he went off alone and turned his face toward Mazandaran.
sentences.s331-s11: Wilderness and darkness and demons and lions — likewise that magic and the brave dragon.
sentences.s331-s12: By those hardships and cares they took the road; (he) reached Mazandaran close to the king.
sentences.s331-s5: When he heard the praises of the Khosrow‑worshipper, he kissed the earth and sprang to his feet.
sentences.s331-s6: Thus he said, 'O sovereign of the world, it is fitting that I have no secret desires.'
sentences.s331-s7: You know what Rostam did in Iran — in war and at banquets, in disgrace and in combat.
sentences.s331-s8: When Kavus set out for Mazandaran — a long road and burdensome parasangs.
sentences.s331-s1: He said to the Iranians, “My moment has risen, and my desire has been renewed.”
sentences.s331-s2: Ask for whatever you ought from me; I have come to scatter the assembly.
sentences.s331-s3: All the elders became wretched and weeping; because of the king’s pain they were consumed (with grief).
sentences.s331-s4: He kept saying, “Whoever you are, O king — Mani, to whom will you make this crown a keepsake?”
sentences.s331-s17: From then on, wherever Kāmos fought, he hurled a man into the clouds.
sentences.s331-s18: I will say a few words about his deeds, for even the tales cannot reach their root.
sentences.s331-s19: If the king grew weary of crown and throne, what would remain for this lion‑hearted, well-wishing man?
sentences.s331-s20: He answered thus: 'His conduct has brought to us toil and his own care.'
sentences.s331-s13: They cut open the flank of the white demon; his inmost heart was (described as) steel, a grain, and a willow.
sentences.s331-s14: Suddenly he tore the head from the body of the sanjeh; his cry rose up to the high cloud.
sentences.s331-s15: When Sohrab the son came into the world, there was no one among the great and noble like him.
sentences.s331-s16: In pursuit of vengeance, King Kavus killed him; from that pain the king wept for years and months.
